posts.body:
New Together Loans review on You Tube. Interesting that is is very low quality. The script is AI generated from a very simple prompt, the voice is robotic, and the visual is just a screen record of someone clicking around the website. They read a random TrustPilot review too. Really nothing of any value here, but It strikes me that this could be done much, much better.

https://youtu.be/5DpNjs8GR88?si=0Ldb_lXCJvCX6m95

options: I don’t think anyone will use this wall

posts.body:
**I’m making this post to test the bold text feature **

If I make a post like this with body text that is not bold I can also add an important word like **wow** in bold

Saturday night with my mate Claude, building a cleaner, smarter collections training classroom that’s easier for newbies to follow and should hopefully save @berginie.botero from losing brain cells scoring modules for 2 hours 😅

comments.body: You're out of usage credits. Buy more to keep going now, or wait until 1:00 AM when your plan usage resets.

Honestly I am on the $200/month plan and it's incredible. i've been on Claude Code keeping it running 12 hours a day or more for the last week and a half and I've never come near to using 10% of the limit.
@james.benamor OK I clicked out of this and posted it too soon and now I've realised we need an Edit button. Building it now!
@james.benamor I will go for the $50 as i want to finish this before monday
@mourad.malki edit button now live. That’s what $200/month buys you. 💰💰💰

@james.benamor  i don't think there is a need for the 'mark all read' button, as a comment should auto be marked read, when its read. I would rather have a button to mark a specific comment unread, so i can get back to it

posts.body: Hey everyone, I built a wall which we can post to instead of Humand. Let me know any features you'd like to see here.

comments.body: yes! this is just about acceptable. woooo!
I can't tag you here
Ah tagging. Nice I forgot about that. I did get a notification because you replied to me, but tagging would be good too
I didn't get one when you replied to my comment
@mourad.malki did you get one now?
@james.benamor

posts.body:
With growth continuing to accelerate in TC I thought I'd start a new feature here.

From now, until we run out of them, we will be tracking the growth of the TC 'big loan' book by looking at stadiums that would NO LONGER hold all of our current borrowers.

First up, Easter Road Stadium, home of Hibernian FC. The stadium has an all-seated capacity of just over 20,000 which makes it the fifth-largest football stadium in Scotland. Easter Road is also known by Hibs fans as "The Holy Ground"

Redevelopment of the stadium began in 1995 and was completed in 2010. The Easter Road pitch had a pronounced slope until it was removed in 2000.

Stay tuned for more updates on Stadiums that now would not hold all of our borrowers.

comments.body: Bad news everyone. 😟 Unfortunately our borrowers now won't fit in Pittodrie Stadium, home of Aberdeen FC, as that stadium only has a maximum capacity of 20,866. If any of you were about to book that stadium for a compulsory all-hands meeting please make alternative arrangements. Aberdeen FC played at various venues within the city, until a former dung hill for police horses was cleared and became the site of Pittodrie in 1899. Pittodrie stadium was the first stadium in the world to have a dugout. In 1978, Pittodrie became the first all-seated stadium in Great Britain.

If after the disappointment of Hibernaian and Aberdeen FC you were hoping that we might have better luck with stadiums closer to home, I have an update that you might not be happy about. Fratton Park, home of Portsmouth FC is now out of the question. At a capacity of only 20,899 it is no longer capable of holding all of our current borrowers. Portsmouth's football ground was deceptively named as "Fratton Park" by the club's founding directors, to persuade supporters that the new Milton-based football ground was within walking distance of neighbouring Fratton's convenient railway station; the true distance between the railway station and football ground is actually over a mile. The site of Fratton Park was formerly a potato field. In 1956 Fratton Park became the first English football ground to stage an evening Football League match under artificial light.

Not sure how to tell you this so soon after the Fratton Park disappointment, but we just grew out of Swansea's Swansea.com stadium. The Swansea.com stadium hosts both rugby and football events, but will now definitely not be hosting a gathering of all of our active borrowers as it only has a capacity of 21,088. The Swansea.com stadium was previously named The Katie Phillips Stadium after a competition which chose a rugby supporter at random to name the stadium after. The stadium was completed in 2005 and cost over £50m to build. Also yay for making it to the first modern-looking mega stadium guys. 👏🏼👏🏼👏🏼

New month, new stadium that will NO LONGER HOLD all of our active borrowers. I'm afraid Vicarage Road is now too small for us, as it only has a max capacity of 21,977 😭 if you were considering Watford's ground as a potential venue for an annual gathering of our customers then, despite Watford's excellent transportation links, you should look elsewhere. Vicarage Road was sold by Watford FC in 2002 when they almost went bankrupt and was later bought back after Elton John held a concert there donating all proceeds to a fan's campaign called ‘Let’s Buy Back The Vic’. One of the 4 stands is now named The Elton John Stand.

Hi everyone 👋 Important NEWS! Overnight last night we grew out of Turf Moor, Burnley FCs stadium, which sadly only has a capacity of 22,546. Please do not attempt to hold a gathering of all our customers at Turf Moor. I repeat: Do not gather our customers at Turf Moor. Turf Moor is the 2nd oldest continuously used football stadium in the UK, and became the first stadium ever to be visited by royalty in 1886 when Queen Victoria's husband, Prince Albert attended a game there. In 1878 an experimental game was played there to prove the benefit of electric lights, but it was a failure due to the 3 lights erected providing insufficient illumination. Most of the crowd left early in disgust.

Oakwell Stadium is now a NO GO for our borrowers. Please, under no circumstances tell them to gather there. It will be a very bad result for a number of reasons, mainly because it has a capacity of only 23,009 people, but also because we have not booked it, or even spoken to the owners once. Oakwell is the home of Barnsley FC and, despite being built in 1887, has survived for almost 140 years without a single thing happening there that is interesting enough for me to mention here. The roof of the West stand is made of corrugated iron apparently. 🤭

They say it’s not the years in your life that count, it’s the life in your years. I sincerely hope this is true, because, due to this relentless growth which we still don’t seem to be able to slow down, we have now grown out of the oldest continuously used football stadium IN THE WORLD 🧑 Deepdale Stadium, the home of Preston North End, is now totally useless to us, having only the capacity to take 23,408 people. Less than the number of active, good borrowers that TC is currently serving. Preston North End was originally a cricket club, but the cricket team took up Football as a winter fitness activity in 1878. They were so good at it that by 1880 the jacked in the cricket and converted to a full time football team. They won the league in 1890, but their early success seems to be beginner’s luck, as they haven’t won anything since. In 1913 there was a terrorist attack on Deepdale by suffragettes (like the ones in Mary Poppins) that attempted to destroy the stadium. The attack was a failure, but the same terrorists succeeded in destroying Arsenal’s ground.

I got back from 10 days away yesterday feeling refreshed and eager to smash all of our goals before the end of the year. Unfortunately, less than an hour into the morning and I'm totally broken at the news that we are now unable to use the modern John Smith's Stadium in Huddersfield. This stadium, which I had been pinning my hopes on for our planned all-borrower get together, takes a whopping 24,500 people, which a couple of weeks ago was more than enough for our requirements, but which our runaway growth has made totally redundant whilst I was away. The John Smiths stadium was built in 1995, making it younger than my attraction to Pamela Anderson. It was the venue for the Rugby League World Cup 4 times, most recently in 2021. Huddersfield Giants, for whom The John Smith Stadium is home ground, were previously based at Fartown, a smaller ground in Huddersfield. When I read this I misread it as FartTown which I thought was hilarious and would make an excellent fact for this post, but since then I have realised there's only 1 't' and it's not really that funny. Sorry.

Umm bad news guys. I know it’s Friday night and I wouldn’t normally raise an issue unless it was really serious. But we just grew out of Edgbaston Cricket Ground 😲 This ground is absolutely MASSIVE. Just look at it. The little worn out bit in the middle has a man who is one of the best people at throwing things in the whole world. He throws a ball as hard as he can. Then there’s another man whose special skill is hitting that ball with a wooden stick, REALLY HARD. He has actually been chosen for being one of the best hitters of things who can hit things the hardest to make them go the furthest. Then the distance between the middle and the first seats in any direction is about the furthest that he can hit the very fast ball. That is a really big circle. Then people sit in that circle on chairs that go higher and higher up in the air. Stadium seats. Rows and rows of the bloody things. It’s SO MANY SEATS. There are SO MANY PEOPLE there. But as of today we have more borrowers. Little us working from our little room, and a few of us in America and other places. We are serving more people than fit in that bloody massive stadium. Every day. 😬

I know what you're thinking "Just book the Brick Community Stadium James. It's HUGE. If it can fit all of the fans of Wigan Athletic, AND Wigan Warriors surely it can fit your little loan company's borrowers in it?" Well, no. As of today it cannot. With a capacity of 25,133 the Brick Community Stadium is too small The Brick community Stadium was the subject of controversy in 2022 when it emerged that one goal was bigger than the other. The search goes on.

I know we might have got used to growing out of stadiums, but we just grew out of an entire sport. Welford Road Stadium is the largest purpose-built Rugby Union stadium in England. Unfortunately despite being the biggest, at a capacity of only 25,849 it isn’t big enough to take all of out current borrowers. 😢🏈👎

At some point last night (UK time) Old Trafford Cricket Ground became non-viable. Despite being England's second oldest Test venue, having hosted the first Ashes tournament in 1884, and having hosted the Cricket World Cup 5 times including 2019 it is now totally useless to us as a gathering place for all of our active borrowers, having only a capacity of 26,000. If we are planning on using a cricket ground at all for our all-borrowers meeting we should hurry up because there are now only 2 left that might fit us.

When I was a small child I remember hearing about a football club called Crystal Palace and fully believing that they must actually play their matches in a huge palace made of crystals, rather like the Ice Palace that Elsa makes for herself in Frozen. I was 47 1/2 when I realised that I never officially corrected that understanding and have never actually seen or heard about the (frankly quite boring) stadium that they actually play in. Selhurst Park was a former brickworks, but is now a football ground that simultaneously disappoints 3 year old me, and 47 year old me by not being a palace made out of crystals, AND only having a capacity of 26,309, making it too small to hold all of our active borrowers.

I know what you're thinking. "James, instead of randomly going through stadiums why not just start with the ones with the prefix 'The' on their names. That way you'll get all the really important, big stadia". Good idea, after all bands like The Doors, The Who, The Sex Pistols, The Beatles, The Rolling Stones are all HUGE. So it may come to a surprise to hear that The Hawthorns, which is one of the 3 largest stadiums with a name starting 'The' is actually too small to fit all of our borrowers, having a capacity of only 26,688. The Hawthorns, home of West Bromwich Albion, was the first stadium built in the 20th Century, and at the time of being built was probably expected to be one of the most important stadiums in the UK, as WBA had just won the FA cup twice and been runners-up three times. In 2017 The Hawthorns was listed by Four-Four-Two magazine as the best stadium in the Premier League. That is a shame because we will now never go there, because it is too small.

New Year, new stadiums that are too small for us. First up, this little thing known as ‘The Oval’. The Oval is the second largest cricket ground in the U.K. during the Second World War it was converted into a Prisoner of War Camp. But it is way too small to hold all of our borrowers, even for a few hours, having a capacity of only 27,500.

Look upon the Toughsheet Community Stadium and despair. For it now hath not the room for our borrowers, being as it is a mere 28,723 in capacity. This grand home of Bolton Wanderers has made wanderers of us, as we continue in our search for a venue that might contain us.

St Andrew's might be the 3rd largest stadium in the UK named after a Saint, but it is not large enough to take all of our current active borrowers. In fact, as I am a bit late to post this, if we did try to fit everyone with a TC loan inside we'd have 29,409 seated, happy people, and over 200 disgruntled, jet lagged Americans stood outside denied admittance. St Andrew's was built in 1906. Just before the first game was held (Birmingham v Middlesbrough first division match) there was heavy snowfall. Rather than cancel the match the staff, including the board of Directors, worked all morning to clear the snow from the pitch by hand. During WW2 the pitch was used as a rifle range to train soldiers before they were shipped out to fight.

We've grown out of the Bet365 Stadium. As well as being the home ground of Stoke City F.C., the 30,089 seater stadium has hosted concerts by Bon Jovi, Bryan Adams, Busted, Elton John and Rod Stewart. Not TC though. TC is too big, and it is too small.

When we moved to Richmond hill from our first office in Ashley Cross we took on a chunk of the 2nd floor of Bristol and West House. We were the first tenants to move in after Portman Building Society moved out. They had been in there whist their new building (now the Nationwide building) was being built. In fact, unlike now, in those days Portman had lots of employees who actually came into work. As a result they had rented and fitted out most of the office space on Richmond Hill. After Bristol and West House we moved into Richmond House and Walton House, which had all also been home to Portman. Portman provided the physical infrastructure: server rooms, network cabling, even left desks behind that we used in practically ever office we occupied. Portman were so important to RG and Richmond Hill that it would have been reasonable to rename the whole road after them. So it makes sense that our first all borrower get-together be held at Portman Road, home of Ipswich Town FC. Unfortunately, you have all ruined this by increasing our active borrowers to 30,240 leaving almost 200 of our customers with nowhere to sit. I hope you're proud of yourselves and your complete disregard for RG history 😡

When I was at school, one day in Geography class they wheeled in a TV and showed us a video about Milton Keynes, City of the Future. Apparently it had been designed and built as a fully new purpose built city. I remember being a bit underwhelmed. It seemed like a city, if the whole city was made by the people who made the Poole Dolphin Centre. Something that isn't underwhelming, and is actually solidly overwhelming though is Stadium MK, the home of Milton Keynes Dons F.C. Built in 2007 with a capacity of 30,500 actual humans. Unfortunately the TC loan book is even more overwhelming (over-overwhelming? superwhelming?) and Stadium MK is, as of last night, too small to contain all of our active borrowers. The stadium was actually built with a roof that is high enough to allow a third tier of seating, extending it to a capacity of over 44,000. So if they get a move on and build that we might be able to outgrow it again in a few months.

City Ground has been home to Nottingham Forest FC for over 125 years and has also hosted Euro 1996 matches. Until 1898 City Ground was called Town Ground. The name was changed when Nottingham was given City status. You can accuse the people of Nottingham of lots of things, but imaginative stadium naming is not one of them. Unfortunately City Ground will now never host TC and our current borrowers, because with a capacity of 30,602 it is way too small.

Shall we just forget about all these little stadiums and go straight for the largest cricket stadium in the UK? The one that is just known as 'Lord's'. That must be MASSIVE right? Well yes, it is. Known as "The Home of Cricket" and dating back to 1787, Lord's is the main venue for international cricket test matches and world cup finals. But it is not big enough to host all of TC's borrowers, because it only takes 31,100 people, and TC has more borrowers than that. 😢

Ewood Park is the home of Blackburn Rovers FC, who have played there since 1890. Before that they were at Oozehead Ground, a piece of farmland that was used to graze cows in between football fixtures. Ewood Park narrowly avoided being destroyed by a terrorist attack by suffragettes in 1913. Unfortunately, with a capacity of only 31,367 there's no way that all of our borrowers can fit in there now, so I suppose that although they failed to destroy the ground, the suffragettes ultimately won in more ways than one 😢

Falmer Stadium, also known as The American Express Stadium, is home to Brighton and Hove Albion. The former stadium was sold by the board of the club back in 1995 leaving the club homeless for what was supposed to be 2-3 years, but ended up being 16 years as a series of planning disputes blocked it being built. In the end permission was granted by John Prescott, then deputy Prime Minister, but his permission was then withdrawn after the local council sued. Eventually permission was granted by the then Secretary of State in 2007 after the council agreed to drop their case. Although it looks like a massive creepy eye, compared to our loan book it is actually quite small, having only 31,876 seats.

The Molineux Stadium has been the home ground of Wolverhampton Wanderers since 1889. At one point it looked like this stadium would not survive, with two of its stands closed on safety grounds, and debts of £1.2m accumulated, the club had slipped to the 4th division, leading to lower attendances and revenue. It was saved by ASDA, who bought the surrounding land and agreed to pay off the club's debts on condition the the local council approved plans for them to build a superstore next door. ASDA's involvement laid the foundations for a resurgence of the club and a series of projects to rebuild the stadium, leading to its current form where it can host up to 32,050 fans. That is way too small for us though, so ASDA probably shouldn't have bothered.
